question.text: Scenario: Mrs. Kavita is 50 years old and plans to retire at 60. She currently earns Rs. 1,50,000 per month and has accumulated Rs. 45 lakh in various retirement-linked investments (EPF: Rs. 20 lakh, PPF: Rs. 10 lakh, NPS: Rs. 15 lakh). Her monthly expenses are Rs. 70,000 (of which Rs. 8,000 is healthcare). She estimates needing Rs. 65,000 per month in today's terms at retirement (reduced due to paid-off home loan). General inflation is 6%, healthcare inflation is 10%, and her pre-retirement portfolio return is 9%. Post-retirement, she expects a 7% nominal return. Her life expectancy is 82 years. Three years ago, she withdrew Rs. 2,00,000 from her EPF for a family emergency. She is considering a step-up SIP strategy for the remaining 10 years and is evaluating whether to invest in an annuity or a systematic withdrawal plan (SWP) at retirement. Question 3 of 5: Mrs. Kavita's existing investments of Rs. 45 lakh are expected to grow at 9% per annum for 10 years. What will be the future value of her existing corpus at retirement, and if she needs a total corpus of Rs. 2.60 crore, how much additional accumulation is required?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** FV of existing investments = Rs. 45,00,000 x (1.09)^10 = Rs. 45,00,000 x 2.3674 = Rs. 1,06,53,289, approximately Rs. 1,06,53,020 with precise computation. Additional corpus needed = Rs. 2,60,00,000 - Rs. 1,06,53,020 = Rs. 1,53,46,980.
**The Trap:** Rs. 85,50,000 uses a lower growth rate (perhaps 7% instead of 9%). Rs. 1,16,38,250 uses 10% growth instead of 9%. Rs. 90,00,000 simply doubles the corpus (a rough mental estimate). The precision in growth rate matters enormously -- 9% vs 10% over 10 years creates a Rs. 10 lakh difference on a Rs. 45 lakh base.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Gap analysis (total corpus needed minus future value of existing investments) is a standard retirement planning step in NISM X-B. This is also tested in CFP and CWM certifications.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When computing the FV of existing investments, use the blended expected return of the actual portfolio, not a single assumed rate. EPF (8.5%), PPF (7.1%), and NPS (9-12% depending on allocation) each contribute differently. A weighted average gives a more accurate projection.
